artifacts.content:
Subject: Juggling Mom, Dad, and everything else

Hey Dan,

Finally sitting down with a lukewarm cup of coffee while Ethan practices trumpet in the living room and Sophie has completely taken over my sketchbook.

I wanted to loop you in on the latest parent logistics. I’m still doing the Mayo runs with Mom on the first and third Thursdays—out the door from Minneapolis around 7:00 a.m., back close to 4:00 p.m. by the time we get through her oncology check-in, labs, and whatever tweaking they’re doing to her chemo at the clinic in Rochester. March 2023 feels like forever ago and also yesterday, doesn’t it? She seems to relax a little when I’m in the room with her oncologist, so I’ll keep doing it unless her schedule changes.

On top of that, I’ve been keeping most Friday afternoons open in case I need to shoot over to Eau Claire for Dad’s PT at Sacred Heart. His post-stroke sessions are still 3:00–4:00 p.m., and with the 2+ hour drive from here I have to leave around 12:30. Some weeks he cancels if he’s wiped out, but I’d rather hold the time than scramble.

I’m trying to layer all this around my client work and the kids’ stuff. Because I’m freelancing, my schedule can go from quiet to chaotic in a day if a big project lands, so I’m constantly rearranging design time between 9:30 and 2:30 on my “work” days. Anything before 10 a.m. is hit or miss for me if I’ve been up late finishing a co-op flyer.

How are things going with your custody mediation? I have to be at Hennepin County Family Court downtown again on the second Monday next month, 10–12, for that check‑in with your lawyer. I’ll text you afterward.

Love you,
Laura

metadata.subject: Juggling Mom, Dad, and everything else

artifacts.content:
Thread with Michael

Laura: Hey, quick question about next Wednesday.

Michael: What’s up?

Laura: I’m trying to figure out if I can squeeze in a volunteer thing late morning and still make my therapy session.

Michael: That’s the 1–2 p.m. video with Dr. Larkin, right?

Laura: Yeah, every other Wednesday since April 2022. LynLake Counseling would probably scold me if I started skipping, given the whole anxiety/panic mess after Grandma Evelyn’s decline.

Michael: So what time is the volunteer thing?

Laura: They were talking about a 10:30–12 activity block with some seniors. I could technically do it, but I’d have to rush home, throw the laptop on the table, and jump straight into telehealth. Not sure that’s great for my nerves.

Michael: Could you ask for a different time? You’ve been better when you have a buffer before and after sessions.

Laura: True. When I have space to decompress, I actually get something out of talking to Dr. Larkin instead of just word‑vomiting about Grandma and Dad and Janet.

Michael: And don’t forget you’re basically on kid duty by 3:00.

Laura: Right. Ethan’s trumpet and Sophie’s soccer. Honestly, part of why I’m even looking at regular senior volunteering is because Dr. Lang at Parkview and I set that goal back in March 2023—find something grounding with older adults after that mild depressive patch when freelance work tanked.

Michael: I remember. Just don’t overbook yourself.

Laura: I won’t. I’ll email them and say Wednesdays before 12:30 are tricky because of my 1–2 slot with Dr. Larkin.

Michael: Sounds good. Need me to grab the kids that day?

Laura: Maybe. I’ll let you know after I see how my week looks.

Subject: Is a weekly volunteer shift even realistic for me?

Hey Anna,

Thanks again for sending the link to that senior community opportunity. I’ve been thinking a lot about whether I can actually commit to something weekly without flaking.

On paper, my Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays look open between school drop‑off and pickup, but in reality my freelance schedule is all over the place. One week I’m leisurely sketching logo ideas at the dining room table, and the next week I’m up until midnight three nights in a row trying to get a rush layout done. Because I work for myself, my schedule can get pretty chaotic with client deadlines, so I can’t really commit to anything super regular with residents and might need to cancel or reschedule at the last minute if a big project comes up. Deadlines tend to pile up unpredictably, and I hate the idea of promising I’ll be there for residents every Wednesday at 11 and then cancelling last‑minute because a big project lands in my inbox.

I keep thinking about why I’m drawn to this in the first place. A lot of it goes back to Grandma Evelyn’s years at Oakwood Hills in Eau Claire (2008–2016). When her dementia got worse around 2013 and she started having those wandering/agitation episodes, I saw how much difference it made when the same calm volunteers were around. Mom and I would show up on weekends, and if one of the familiar faces from the memory care unit was there, Grandma settled so much faster.

And then there’s Janet—Fall 2020 at Brookside in Edina is still burned in my brain. Remember when Michael and I found her alone and crying in the lounge, and later learned she’d gone 3–4 days without anyone really sitting down to talk? We ended up filing that complaint with the director on October 12, 2020. That’s the opposite of what I want residents to experience.

Anyway, part of me thinks even twice a month would be better than nothing, as long as I’m honest about my flaky designer schedule.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Is a weekly volunteer shift even realistic for me?

artifacts.content:
Private journal – 2025-01-30

I’ve been circling around this idea of spending more time with seniors again. Part of me is excited; part of me is terrified I’ll slide back into the emotional exhaustion I felt visiting Grandma at Oakwood Hills.

She was there from 2008 until she died in 2016, and those last few years after the 2013 dementia diagnosis were rough. The wandering, the agitation, weekends of helping Mom and staff calm her down. It was meaningful but also draining. I still remember leaving Eau Claire on Sunday nights feeling hollow. I’ve never actually volunteered directly with seniors before—everything I know about assisted living is really just from those trips to Eau Claire and sitting with Grandma.

So why am I drawn back into that world? Maybe I’m trying to rewrite the story—to see if I can be around residents in assisted living without shutting down. I’d like to get more comfortable in that setting instead of bracing myself every time I walk into a memory care unit. Visiting Grandma’s friends back then, I often felt awkward, searching for something to talk about beyond the weather and “How are you feeling?” and I still don’t feel like I have some magical list of conversation starters.

There’s also the practical side: I’m between a couple of bigger client projects right now, so I have more daytime space than usual. Filling some of that with consistent time at a place like The Waters seems healthier than doom‑scrolling or obsessively reorganizing my Procreate brushes.

And, if I’m honest, part of me is curious about whether the stories and relationships I’d encounter there might feed into my design work—especially the aging and end‑of‑life projects I’ve been gravitating toward. Not in a creepy way, but in the sense that real conversations with older adults could deepen the way I approach those campaigns.

Still, I need to watch my boundaries and not pretend this is purely altruistic. It’s mixed, and that has to be okay.

metadata.title: Journal – seniors & why this pulls at me

artifacts.content:
Event: Monthly court check‑in for Dan + Mom/Dad care blocks

Date: Monday, 2025-02-10
Time: 8:30 a.m.–3:00 p.m.
Location: Morning – home; 10–12 Hennepin County Family Court; afternoon – home office
Attendees: Laura

Details:

8:30–9:15 a.m. – Quiet work block after kids’ drop‑off. Triage email, especially anything from Linden Hills Co‑op about next month’s flyer. Don’t start anything deep.

9:15–9:45 a.m. – Drive downtown, park near Hennepin County Family Court.

10:00 a.m.–12:00 p.m. – Monthly mediation check‑in for Daniel’s custody situation. Be there for Mom if she comes, take notes so Dan doesn’t have to remember every detail himself. Last one was tense; bring water and a granola bar.

12:00–12:30 p.m. – Drive home, decompress in the car (podcast or silence).

12:30–1:30 p.m. – Light admin work only: send update email to Mom and Dan, reschedule any client calls that accidentally landed on first/third Thursdays (Mayo days) or Friday afternoons (possible Eau Claire PT run for Dad at Sacred Heart, 3–4 p.m.).

1:30–3:00 p.m. – Design block if brain allows.

Notes:

– Remember ongoing first and third Thursday commitment: leave Minneapolis ~7:00 a.m. with Mom for Mayo Clinic in Rochester, back ~4:00 p.m. for her oncology/chemo follow‑ups (since March 2023).

– Keep most Friday afternoons flexible in case Dad’s post‑stroke PT in Eau Claire is on; it’s a 2+ hour drive each way.

– Do NOT book any volunteer commitments during 10–12 on second Mondays.

Google Doc – Volunteering + boundaries brainstorm

Date: 2025-02-03

What I’m genuinely excited about:

– One‑on‑one visits: coffee/tea, listening to stories. I know from Redeemer’s memory café days (2019–2022) how rich those conversations can be. I still think about Harold, whose wife told me the police had to bring him home after he wandered from their condo near Lake Calhoun. Sitting with him at the reminiscence art table in the church basement taught me a lot about gentle redirection.

– Light creative stuff: simple watercolor, card‑making, seasonal door decorations. Totally in my wheelhouse.

– Reading aloud, letter‑writing, maybe some music sing‑alongs (old hymns, folk songs).

What I’m NOT up for:

– Anything that feels like nursing or personal care. I’m happy to hand someone a cup of coffee or help steady a bingo card (like I did with Carl when Lisa asked me to sit with him after his early Parkinson’s diagnosis from Mayo back in Summer 2014), but I don’t want to be responsible for transfers, toileting, or anything that requires special training. That’s a hard boundary.

– Being the only person in charge of a large group. I can co‑lead, but I don’t want to be the default cruise director.

Other considerations:

– My freelance workload swings wildly. I need a setup where it’s okay if I occasionally have to swap a shift when a big Linden Hills Co‑op project lands.

– Emotional stamina: I still carry memories of Grandma’s wandering episodes at Oakwood Hills and Janet’s rough stint at Brookside in 2020. I should build in decompression time after visits.

Overall, I want something relational and creative, not medical.

metadata.title: Volunteering with seniors – what fits/what doesn’t

artifacts.content:
Subject: Catch‑up + why I’m leaning toward senior volunteering

Hi Megan,

Long overdue update from the land of trumpet practice and half‑finished design files.

Life is its usual juggling act—kids, clients, and parents all in the air at once. Dad’s recovery from the stroke last June has been a whole saga. Most Friday afternoons I leave my calendar semi‑blank in case I need to drive to Eau Claire for his PT at Sacred Heart (sessions are usually 3–4 p.m.). Between that and Mom’s Mayo oncology days on the first and third Thursdays, I feel like I live on I‑94.

I’ve also been thinking a lot about your caregiving group and where I fit. Most of my hands‑on experience with seniors lately has been family: those 45‑minute Zoom speech practice sessions with Janet when she was in the transitional care unit at Methodist in St. Louis Park during the winter of 2020–21, and then continuing the same memory prompts at her condo in Edina through May 2021. Before that, there were the Saturdays I blocked off in 2021 when Dad had his minor stroke—4–5 hours every weekend at the rehab facility in Eau Claire, just playing cards and listening to stories, because staff were too slammed to sit.

All of that is part of why I’m drawn to a place like The Waters. I want to keep doing that kind of companionship, but in a more structured way, not just when a crisis hits our family.

On the practical side, I’m in a bit of a lull between bigger retainer projects, so I have some extra bandwidth during school hours. It feels better to pour that into something people‑focused than to obsess over LinkedIn.

Do you think the group would mind if my schedule is a little weird because of all the medical stuff and kid logistics?

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Catch‑up + why I’m leaning toward senior volunteering

artifacts.content:
Journal – 2025-02-15

I’m restless again. When things get too crowded in my head—client deadlines, kids’ schedules, the constant low‑grade worry about Mom’s cancer and Dad’s stroke—I start fantasizing about just leaving town for a few days. It’s become a pattern: every few months I book a last‑minute long weekend somewhere quiet (Duluth, a little cabin near Lanesboro, once even a cheap flight to Denver) just to breathe and reset.

The downside is it makes me hesitant to over‑commit to anything too rigid. I like the idea of a weekly volunteer slot with seniors, but I know myself—if I hit that burned‑out point, I might grab a spontaneous week away and need to cancel. I don’t want to be the flaky person who disappears on residents.

I keep coming back to how grounding it felt to sit with Dad for hours in rehab after his minor stroke in June 2021, or to walk Ruth from the Linden Hills senior lunch to the 46th & Upton bus stop and wait 10–15 minutes with her after her new anti‑anxiety meds from Hennepin made her lightheaded. Those simple, present moments with older adults pull me out of my own spiral in a way nothing else does.

Maybe the answer is to start small—one or two visits a month at The Waters, with clear communication about my weird schedule. And keep the solo getaways, but plan them instead of bolting.

Also note: still not thrilled about early mornings. Late‑night co‑op flyer marathons plus 7 a.m. anything do not mix.

metadata.title: Restlessness, travel, and what I can actually promise

artifacts.content:
From: laura.kensington@gmail.com
To: sarah.volunteercoord@lindenhillscc.org
Date: March 4, 2024
Subject: Tuesday evening availability & senior visits

Hi Sarah,

I wanted to follow up about the new senior companion slots you mentioned for Tuesday afternoons and evenings.

Most weeks I’m pretty flexible, but there’s one recurring thing I need to work around: since January 2024 I’ve had a standing rush layout job for Linden Hills Community Co‑op on the last Tuesday evening of every month. I’m the one who finalizes their printed sales flyer, and I block off about 6:30–10:00 p.m. to get the files polished and the final PDFs uploaded. It’s remote design work from my apartment here in Minneapolis, but it’s intense, and the co‑op pays $450 for that monthly rush layout project, so I try not to schedule anything else that day that might run late.

Because of that, I’d prefer not to take a late‑afternoon or evening volunteer shift on those specific Tuesdays in case the flyer project runs long or I need a quick nap beforehand. Earlier in the day on those dates could still work, and any other Tuesdays are wide open.

I’m especially interested in visits with older adults who may be a bit vulnerable. Back in Fall 2016 I visited a woman named Helen through Redeemer Lutheran Church’s homebound ministry, and that experience really shaped how I think about boundaries and support with seniors (especially around money topics).

Let me know if that limitation still fits your scheduling needs.

Best,
Laura

Google Doc – Volunteering with Seniors – Personal Reflection
Last edited: February 12, 2024
Author: Laura Marie Kensington

I’ve been thinking about why I keep gravitating toward intergenerational stuff and seniors in particular.

A big turning point was Fall 2016, when I started visiting Helen through Redeemer Lutheran Church’s homebound ministry. One afternoon in late October 2016, after a visit at her apartment near 50th & Xerxes in Minneapolis, Pastor Mark pulled me aside in the hallway. He explained that Helen’s son had just filed for financial guardianship because her early dementia had led to a few incidents of writing out large checks to distant relatives—one was for $5,000. Pastor Mark gently asked me to quietly redirect any conversations about money or “helping people out” when I was with her. That pastoral conversation about money boundaries really stayed with me.

Since then, I’ve tried to be especially alert to signs of financial exploitation or confusion when I’m with older adults. I focus on safe topics—photos, Psalms, puzzles, family stories—and avoid anything that might feel like pressure around giving or donations. I’m also realizing I’m better suited to relational, conversational roles than to anything medical or physical; I’m not very comfortable with hands-on or personal-care tasks, and I’d rather keep it to simple things like pouring coffee, walking slowly beside someone, or helping with a crossword. It’s a balance between honoring their autonomy and recognizing when someone else (like a guardian or adult child) has stepped in for protection.

As I look at new volunteer roles, I want to bring that experience forward: listening well, watching for red flags, and remembering that my role is companionship, not informal financial advisor. When I picture what fits with my life in Minneapolis right now—between school drop-offs, client work at my little dining-room desk, and trips to see my parents in Eau Claire—it’s these steady, low-key visits and conversations that feel both sustainable and meaningful.

Event title: Linden Hills Co‑op Flyer Rush – Blocked Work Night
Date: Tuesday, April 30, 2024
Time: 6:30–10:00 p.m.
Location: Home office – 4829 Sheridan Avenue S, Apt 3B, Minneapolis, MN 55410
Attendees: Laura Marie Kensington

Description / Notes:

Monthly rush layout job for Linden Hills Community Co‑op – primary client. Since January 2024, I’ve been doing this the last Tuesday evening of every month as a standing gig.

• Typical window: 3.5 hours of focused layout and proofing (6:30–10:00 p.m.) to finalize the co‑op’s printed sales flyer.
• Deliverables: final packaged InDesign files + press‑ready PDFs uploaded to their printer.
• Rate: $450 per monthly rush layout project.

Block the entire evening – NO other commitments. Do not schedule:
– Late‑afternoon volunteering
– School or church meetings
– Social stuff that might run past 5:00 p.m.

Plan:
– Quiet hour from 5:00–6:00 p.m. to rest, light dinner with Michael and the kids.
– Quick walk or stretch at 6:00 p.m., then coffee/tea.
– Log on by 6:25 p.m. so I can jump as soon as the final price changes and product list come in from the Linden Hills Community Co‑op marketing manager.

Reminder: This is an important piece of monthly income, so treat it like an in‑office night shift.
